topIssues.title: vulkan: Qwen2.5-14B Q4_K_M bandwidth-efficiency cliff on Lunar Lake iGPU (n=1, kernel-selection suspected)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Summary

`Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct Q4_K_M` (~8.5 GB) runs at **1.15 t/s tg** on Intel Arc 140V iGPU via Shaw's Vulkan + W4-B kernels — only ~22% of the LPDDR5X bandwidth ceiling, vs 63-87% on the other Llama / Qwen3-family Q4_K_M models I tested with the **same Vulkan build, same drivers, same `-ngl 99 -c 2048` invocation**.

**n=1 caveat upfront**: I have only tested *Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct Q4_K_M* in the 14B param class on this hardware. The cliff may be Qwen2.5-14B-specific (head-dim / GQA-factor / tile-alignment) rather than a general "14B param-class" issue. A second 14B (Phi-4-14B, Mistral-Nemo-12B) would tell us which. I'm filing it now because the gap is large enough to be worth investigating even at n=1, and the diagnostic ask is the same either way.

## Bandwidth ceiling derivation

For a memory-bandwidth-bound decoder (the steady-state regime on integrated GPUs with Q4_K_M weights), every token needs ~one full pass of the weight bytes. So:

```
ceiling_t_per_s ≈ sustained_bandwidth_GB_per_s / weights_GB
```

For Lunar Lake on-package LPDDR5X-8533:
- Theoretical peak: 8533 MT/s × 16 B/transfer = 136.5 GB/s
- Sustained iGPU-effective (after refresh, CPU-shared, page traffic): **~75 GB/s** (Intel-published ~67 GB/s sustained; rounded up slightly for cool-laptop, fresh-server best case)

So the ceiling-t/s column below is `75 / weights_GB`. The measured t/s is `timings.predicted_per_second` from `/v1/chat/completions`. Efficiency is `measured / ceiling`.

## Bench (cool laptop, no contention, same prompt, fresh server per row)

| Model | Q4_K_M weights | 75 GB/s ceiling | Measured tg | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct | 1.9 GB | 39.5 t/s | 20.0 t/s | **51%** |
| Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct | 4.9 GB | 15.3 t/s | 5.7 t/s | **37%** |
| Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct Q3_K_M | 3.8 GB | 19.7 t/s | 6.3 t/s | 32% |
| **Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct** | **8.5 GB** | **8.8 t/s** | **1.15 t/s** | **13%** |
| Qwen3.6-27B Q4_K_M (body-only, DFlash disabled) | 16 GB | 4.7 t/s | 1.15 t/s | 25% |

(Earlier draft of this issue used a more optimistic 50% sustained-headroom assumption that bumped the column to a "60-87% efficiency" framing. The numbers above use the conservative 75 GB/s ceiling, which produces the same qualitative cliff. Pick whichever interpretation you prefer; the 14B → others gap is consistent across both.)

The cleanest cliff is **3B → 8B → 14B = 51% → 37% → 13%**. The 27B suffers from the SWA spec-decode gate too ([#7635](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/issues/7635)) which masks its true efficiency, so it is excluded from the cliff trend.

## Repro

Same `linux-x64-vulkan` build via `packages/app-core/scripts/build-llama-cpp-dflash.mjs`:

```bash
LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan \
~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan/llama-server \
  -m <model>.gguf \
  -ngl 99 -c 2048 --host 127.0.0.1 --port 31xxx
```

Capture `timings.predicted_per_second` from `/v1/chat/completions`.

## Why this matters

This is the difference between "Qwen2.5-14B is the obvious next quality step from Llama-3.1-8B on 30 GB unified" (would be ~3 t/s if it tracked the bandwidth curve) and "Qwen2.5-14B is actively unusable" (1.15 t/s is below human-perceptible chat rate). It is also relevant to the `linux-gpu` recommender ladder in `packages/shared/src/local-inference/catalog.ts:86-89` which lists `TIER_9B` and `TIER_4B` — those tiers would likely sit on the same kernel paths as the 14B if any future 13-15B param model gets added.

## Hypothesis (untested)

Qwen2.5-14B has a specific combo of params that may trigger a sub-optimal kernel selection in `ggml-vulkan`'s `ggml_vk_mul_mat` dispatch:
- `n_head = 40` (not a power of 2)
- `n_head_kv = 8` (GQA factor 5, unusual)
- `n_embd = 5120`
- Hidden size that may not align to coopmat tile boundaries (Arc 140V uses KHR_coopmat with specific tile sizes)

Shaw's W4-B Vulkan kernels (`turbo3`, `qjl_full`, `polarquant`) target Qwen3.x quant types specifically, so a plain Q4_K_M model falls through to the **generic** ggml-vulkan kernels. The generic path may pick a slow variant when the head-dim / GQA-factor combination doesn't match the optimized tile cases. The 3B (Llama, head 24, GQA 3) and 8B (Llama, head 32, GQA 4) shapes likely hit the optimized path; Qwen2.5-14B's shape may not.

## Diagnostic asks

1. Capture the Vulkan kernel-selection log under `GGML_VK_DEBUG=1 GGML_VK_DEBUG_DISPATCH=1` for both Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-14B running the same prompt. Diff the kernel names invoked. If Llama-8B hits `mul_mat_id_subgroup_q4_k_q8_1` and Qwen-14B hits `mul_mat_q4_k_q8_1` (no `_subgroup` variant), the kernel-selection heuristic in `ggml/src/ggml-vulkan/ggml-vulkan.cpp` is biased toward Llama-shaped models.

2. Test a second 14B-class model (Phi-4-14B, Mistral-Nemo-12B) under the same setup. If it sits closer to the 8B efficiency, the cliff is Qwen2.5-specific. If it also drops to ~13%, it is a 14B-param-class kernel-selection issue.

3. Check if `--n-cpu-moe` or moving a few layers to CPU would be faster — i.e., the Vulkan kernel is slower than AVX-VNNI for these op sizes.

## Related

- [#7634](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/pull/7634) — full bench dataset for this hardware class
- [#7635](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/issues/7635) — SWA-on-27B spec-decode failure (different root cause; relevant because 27B's efficiency in the table above is also bandwidth-related)
- [#7636](https://github.com/elizaOS/eliza/issues/7636) — Shaw-merge tracker; some Vulkan kernels may have been touched by the upstream merge

## Test environment

- Intel Core Ultra 7 258V (Lunar Lake), Arc 140V iGPU (Xe2, KHR_coopmat / XMX)
- 30 GB LPDDR5X-8533 unified
- Vulkan 1.3.295, glslc shaderc 2026.1-1+b1, ANV driver
- Submodule HEAD: `d629a3768` (`milady/main`) + my 5 fix commits on `nubs/swa-spec-decode-probe-fallback` (none of them touch Vulkan kernels)
- Cool laptop (73-75 °C), no concurrent processes, fresh server start per row

topIssues.title: speculative decoding: M-RoPE position validation rejects cross-drafter Qwen3.5 batches (acceptance collapses to 9%, tg drops below 1 t/s)
topIssues.author: NubsCarson
topIssues.body:
## Summary

When `llama-speculative-simple` runs a Qwen3.5 body + a same-vocab Qwen3.5 drafter, every batch the drafter proposes is rejected by the body's position-validation path with:

```
init: the tokens of sequence 0 in the input batch have inconsistent sequence positions:
 - the last position stored in the memory module of the context (i.e. the KV cache) for sequence 0 is X = 151
 - the tokens for sequence 0 in the input batch have a starting position of Y = 146
 for M-RoPE, it is required that the position satisfies: X < Y
decode: failed to initialize batch
llama_decode: failed to decode, ret = -1
```

These errors fire continuously throughout the generation. Spec-decode degenerates: acceptance falls to **9.5%** (21/222 drafts), effective tg drops to **0.33 t/s** (vs ~3-4 t/s body-only would give). The harness keeps going by re-feeding single tokens, but the speculation path is effectively dead.

## Why this matters

Qwen3.5 uses multimodal RoPE (M-RoPE) positions even for text-only inference. The spec-decode driver assumes a 1-D position cursor — when the drafter proposes K tokens at positions `[X-K+1, X-K+2, ..., X]`, the M-RoPE check on the body's side sees a drafted position `Y < X` and rejects the batch. This means **any** Qwen3.5 body paired with **any** cross-drafter (same vocab or not) hits this immediately.

This blocks both:

- **End-user DIY spec-decode** (Qwen3.5-9B body + Qwen3.5-0.8B drafter, both from `unsloth/Qwen3.5-*-GGUF`).
- **Shaw's intended Eliza-1 flow** when distilled Qwen3.5 drafters land — same code path, same M-RoPE check.

Reported here because the planned recommender ladder (per the 2026-05-12 directive in `packages/shared/src/local-inference/catalog.ts`) routes most users to Qwen3.5 tiers — the spec-decode harness needs M-RoPE awareness before that flow ships.

## Repro

Linux x64, Intel Arc 140V iGPU, this fork at `v1.2.0-eliza` (`a61c93aaa5`), Vulkan build via `build-llama-cpp-dflash.mjs --target linux-x64-vulkan` with the glslc fix from #7626 applied.

```bash
LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan \\
~/.eliza/local-inference/bin/dflash/linux-x64-vulkan/llama-speculative-simple \\
  -m Qwen3.5-9B-Q4_K_M.gguf \\
  -md Qwen3.5-0.8B-Q8_0.gguf \\
  --draft-min 2 --draft-max 8 --draft-p-min 0.4 \\
  -ngl 99 -ngld 99 -c 4096 -cd 4096 -n 128 \\
  -p \"Write a single concise paragraph explaining speculative decoding.\"
```

Output:

```
encoded   19 tokens in    1.046 seconds, speed:   18.167 t/s
decoded  129 tokens in  388.786 seconds, speed:    0.332 t/s

n_draft   = 8
n_predict = 129
n_drafted = 222
n_accept  = 21
accept    = 9.459%
```

The `init: the tokens of sequence 0...` error block repeats ~30 times during generation.

GGUFs used:

- Body: `unsloth/Qwen3.5-9B-GGUF/Qwen3.5-9B-Q4_K_M.gguf` (sha matches upstream)
- Drafter: `unsloth/Qwen3.5-0.8B-GGUF/Qwen3.5-0.8B-Q8_0.gguf`

Both carry the Qwen3.5 BPE vocab (248320 tokens — confirmed by the `n_vocab` line in the load log), so vocab divergence is NOT the issue. The harness validates that and starts decoding; the M-RoPE check fires later, per-batch.

## Where the check lives

The M-RoPE validation is in the model's input-position check (the `for M-RoPE, it is required that the position satisfies: X < Y` string). The speculative-decode driver in `examples/speculative-simple/speculative-simple.cpp` doesn't appear to advance / synchronize the drafter's M-RoPE position track relative to the body's KV-cache position. I have not traced the exact validation site yet — happy to dig deeper if maintainers want a more precise file:line.

## Suggested fixes (any subset)

1. **Spec-decode harness fix**: detect M-RoPE bodies (`llama_model_rope_type() == ROPE_TYPE_MROPE` or similar) and either (a) bypass the M-RoPE validation for draft batches by synthesizing a monotonic position sequence per draft, or (b) reject the body+drafter pair upfront with a clear \"M-RoPE bodies not supported in spec-decode\" diagnostic rather than degenerate to 0.3 t/s silently.

2. **Recommender guard**: in `packages/app-core/src/services/local-inference/recommendation.ts`, tag Qwen3.5/3.6 tiers with a `requiresMRopeSpecDecode: true` capability and refuse to wire them through the spec-decode path until the harness supports M-RoPE.

3. **Per-tier kernel-caps check**: `target-meta.json` already has a `kernelCaps.required` array (`turboquant_q3, qjl, polarquant, dflash`). Add `mrope-spec-decode` to the list for Qwen3.5/3.6 tiers and have the runtime gate on it.

## Related

- #7629 — Qwen3.5/3.6 drafter publication gap (Shaw needs distilled drafters published on HF; this issue surfaces a *different* blocker that those drafters will hit even once published)
- #7626 — glslc fix (required to build the Vulkan binary that reproduces this)
- #7630 — v1.2.0-eliza submodule bump (reproduced on this submodule sha)

## Note

I do not have a CUDA host. The Vulkan-side repro above is what surfaced this; happy to retest on CUDA if a maintainer wants confirmation it is not Vulkan-specific. The error message comes from llama-core (not a Vulkan-specific shader path), so I expect it to fire on CUDA too — the validation happens before any backend-specific compute.

topIssues.title: cloud-api: non-streaming /v1/chat/completions returns 500 when the ai_billing_records insert fails
topIssues.author: jqmwa
topIssues.body:
## Summary

A non-streaming `POST /api/v1/chat/completions` against Eliza Cloud currently fails for every request. The model inference succeeds and credits are reconciled, but the request still returns **HTTP 500** because the durable `ai_billing_records` audit-row insert throws — and the non-streaming handler treats that as fatal.

Three issues are bundled here:

1. The non-streaming handler treats an audit-record write failure as fatal — **inconsistent with the streaming path**, which swallows the identical failure.
2. The `ai_billing_records` INSERT fails — the underlying Postgres cause is not surfaced, so it can't be diagnosed from outside.
3. The API leaks raw SQL + bind params (including internal UUIDs) in the error body.

## Environment

- Eliza Cloud — `https://www.elizacloud.ai/api/v1/chat/completions`
- Non-streaming (`stream` unset / `false`)
- Model-independent — reproduced with `openai/gpt-4o-mini`, `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5`, `google/gemini-2.5-flash`, `google/gemini-2.0-flash-001`
- 100% reproducible, including the first request on a freshly-funded org

## Reproduction

```bash
curl -sS https://www.elizacloud.ai/api/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"openai/gpt-4o-mini","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"hi"}]}'
```

Response (HTTP 500), with the org/user UUIDs redacted:

```json
{"error":{"message":"Failed query: insert into \"ai_billing_records\" (\"id\", \"organization_id\", \"user_id\", \"usage_record_id\", \"reservation_transaction_id\", \"settlement_transaction_ids\", \"idempotency_key\", \"request_id\", \"provider\", \"model\", \"billing_source\", \"pricing_snapshot_ids\", \"provider_request_id\", \"provider_instance_id\", \"provider_endpoint\", \"usage_total_cost\", \"ledger_total\", \"status\", \"metadata\", \"created_at\") values (default, $1, ... $18, default) on conflict (\"organization_id\",\"idempotency_key\") do nothing returning ...\nparams: <org_id>,<user_id>,<usage_record_id>,<reservation_txn_id>,[\"<settlement_txn_id>\"],<idempotency_key>,<request_id>,openai,openai/gpt-4o-mini,openrouter,[],,,,0.000007,0.000007,recorded,{...}","type":"api_error"}}
```

The `metadata` param shows billing already completed before this insert: `"reservation":{"reservedAmount":0.00054,"actualCost":0.000007,"adjustmentType":"refund"}` — inference and credit settlement both succeeded.

## Analysis

### 1. Non-streaming handler aborts on audit-row failure

In `packages/cloud-api/v1/chat/completions/route.ts`, `handleNonStreamingRequest()`:

```ts
try {
  const result = await generateText({ ... });                       // inference OK
  const billing = await billUsage(billingContext, result.usage);
  const reconciliation = await settleReservation(billing.totalCost); // credits settled
  ...
  const usageRecord = await recordUsageAnalytics(billingContext, billing, { ... });
  if (usageRecord) {
    await aiBillingRecordsService.record({ ... });                   // <-- throws
  }
  ...
  return addCorsHeaders(Response.json({ /* completion */ }));
} catch (error) {
  await settleReservation(0);   // re-settles an already-settled reservation
  throw error;                  // -> 500, the completed response is discarded
}
```

When `aiBillingRecordsService.record()` throws:

- the already-generated completion is discarded and the caller gets a 500;
- `settleReservation(0)` runs a **second time**, after `settleReservation(billing.totalCost)` already ran for the same reservation.

The **streaming** path handles the identical call differently — `streamText({ onFinish })` wraps `aiBillingRecordsService.record()` in its own `try/catch` that logs and continues:

```ts
onFinish: async ({ text, usage }) => {
  try {
    ...
    if (usageRecord) {
      await aiBillingRecordsService.record({ ... });
    }
    ...
  } catch (error) {
    await settleReservation(0);
    logger.error("[Chat Completions] onFinish error", { ... });
  }
}
```

The schema doc comment describes `ai_billing_records` as a reconciliation/audit join record, so the streaming behavior (log + continue) looks like the intended one. The non-streaming path should not fail a request — and discard a paid-for completion — because an audit row didn't persist.

### 2. The INSERT itself fails (root cause not visible)

`AiBillingRecordsRepository.createDeduped()` in `packages/cloud-shared/src/db/repositories/ai-billing-records.ts`:

```ts
.insert(aiBillingRecords).values(data)
.onConflictDoNothing({ target: [aiBillingRecords.organization_id, aiBillingRecords.idempotency_key] })
.returning();
```

The API surfaces Drizzle's `Failed query: ...` wrapper message but not the underlying `pg` error (`error.cause`), so the real reason is invisible from outside. Since it is 100% reproducible from the first request, plausible causes:

- the `ai_billing_records_org_idempotency_unique` unique index is missing in the deployed DB, so `ON CONFLICT ("organization_id","idempotency_key")` matches no constraint (Postgres `42P10`). Migration `0118_add_ai_billing_and_alert_events.sql` creates it with `CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS`, so a partially-applied migration would produce exactly this.
- a foreign-key violation on `usage_record_id` or `reservation_transaction_id` if the referenced row isn't visible to the writer connection.

Logging `error.cause` alongside the Drizzle wrapper would make this self-diagnosable.

### 3. Information disclosure

The error response returns the full SQL statement, every bind parameter, and internal UUIDs (organization_id, user_id, usage_record_id, transaction IDs) to the API client. DB-layer errors should be logged server-side and returned to clients as a generic message.

## Impact

Non-streaming chat completions are currently unusable on Eliza Cloud — every call 500s after the model has already run and credits have been settled.

topIssues.title: chore: configure release credentials for full cross-platform OTA pipeline
topIssues.author: lalalune
topIssues.body:
## Overview

The cross-platform OTA and release CI pipeline is implemented but requires the following secrets and configuration to be added to the repo before each channel can publish. This issue tracks what's needed.

**Where to add secrets:** GitHub repo Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret.

---

## Already configured (existing channels working)

These secrets should already be present from prior setup:

| Secret | Used by |
|---|---|
| `CSC_LINK` | macOS code signing (Electrobun) |
| `CSC_KEY_PASSWORD` | macOS code signing |
| `APPLE_ID` | Apple notarization + App Store |
| `APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD` | Apple notarization + App Store |
| `APPLE_TEAM_ID` | Apple signing |
| `ITC_TEAM_ID` | iTunes Connect team |
| `APP_STORE_APP_ID` | App Store Connect app ID |
| `MATCH_PASSWORD` | iOS cert repo (fastlane match) |
| `MATCH_GIT_URL` | iOS cert repo URL |
| `MATCH_GIT_BASIC_AUTHORIZATION` | iOS cert repo auth |
| `MAS_CSC_LINK` | Mac App Store signing cert |
| `MAS_CSC_KEY_PASSWORD` | Mac App Store cert password |
| `MAS_INSTALLER_CERT` | Mac App Store installer cert |
| `MAS_INSTALLER_KEY_PASSWORD` | Installer cert password |
| `APP_STORE_API_KEY_ID` | App Store Connect API key |
| `APP_STORE_API_ISSUER_ID` | App Store Connect API issuer |
| `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_BASE64` | Android signing keystore |
| `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD` | Keystore password |
| `ANDROID_KEY_ALIAS` | Key alias |
| `ANDROID_KEY_PASSWORD` | Key password |
| `PLAY_STORE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON` | Google Play Store upload |
| `WINDOWS_SIGN_CERT_BASE64` | Windows Authenticode signing |
| `WINDOWS_SIGN_CERT_PASSWORD` | Windows cert password |
| `WINDOWS_SIGN_TIMESTAMP_URL` | Timestamp server URL |
| `ELIZAOS_CLOUD_API_KEY` | elizaOS Cloud OTA manifest publish (also aliased as `ELIZACLOUD_API_KEY`) |
| `ELIZAOS_CLOUD_BASE_URL` | elizaOS Cloud API base URL |
| `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` | Release verification / AI-assisted steps |

---

## Missing — required for new channels

### Windows Store (Microsoft Partner Center)

**Workflow:** `windows-store-release.yml` (called from `release-all.yml`)

| Secret | Description |
|---|---|
| `MS_TENANT_ID` | Azure AD tenant ID |
| `MS_CLIENT_ID` | Azure AD app (service principal) client ID |
| `MS_CLIENT_SECRET` | Azure AD app client secret |
| `MS_APP_ID` | Microsoft Store app ID (from Partner Center) |

**How to get these:**

1. **Register the app in Partner Center** (if not already):
   - Go to https://partner.microsoft.com → Windows & Xbox → Overview → New product → App
   - Note the **Store ID** (format: `9XXXXXXXXXXXXXXX`) — this is `MS_APP_ID`

2. **Create an Azure AD app for CI:**
   - Go to https://portal.azure.com → Azure Active Directory → App registrations → New registration
   - Name: `elizaos-store-ci`, Supported account types: Single tenant
   - After creation, note the **Application (client) ID** → `MS_CLIENT_ID`
   - Note the **Directory (tenant) ID** → `MS_TENANT_ID`
   - Go to Certificates & secrets → New client secret → note the value → `MS_CLIENT_SECRET`

3. **Link the Azure AD app to Partner Center:**
   - Partner Center → Account settings → User management → Azure AD applications → Add Azure AD application
   - Find your app, assign role **Manager** (needs submission create/commit permissions)

4. **Verify** by running `windows-store-release.yml` with `submit_to_store: false` first to confirm the MSIX builds correctly before attempting a real submission.

---

### Snap Store

**Workflow:** `snap-publish.yml` (called from `release-all.yml`)

| Secret | Description |
|---|---|
| `SNAPCRAFT_STORE_CREDENTIALS` | Snapcraft login token for CI uploads |

**How to get:**

1. Create a Snap Store account at https://snapcraft.io/
2. Register the snap name (run locally, once):
   ```bash
   snapcraft login
   snapcraft register elizaos-app
   ```
3. Generate CI credentials:
   ```bash
   snapcraft export-login \
     --snaps elizaos-app \
     --acls package_upload,package_push,package_register \
     --expires "2030-01-01T00:00:00" \
     snap-store-credentials.txt
   cat snap-store-credentials.txt
   ```
4. Copy the output (base64 blob) → add as `SNAPCRAFT_STORE_CREDENTIALS`
5. Delete `snap-store-credentials.txt` locally

---

### Debian apt Repository

**Workflow:** `publish-apt-repo.yml` (called from `release-all.yml`)

| Secret | Description |
|---|---|
| `DEBIAN_GPG_PRIVATE_KEY` | Armored GPG private key for signing the apt repo |
| `DEBIAN_GPG_KEY_ID` | 16-character GPG key ID (used by reprepro to identify which key to sign with) |
| `DEBIAN_GPG_PASSPHRASE` | Passphrase for the GPG key (optional — leave empty if generating without one) |

**How to get:**

1. Generate a dedicated signing key (do NOT use a personal key):
   ```bash
   gpg --batch --gen-key <<KEYEOF
   Key-Type: RSA
   Key-Length: 4096
   Subkey-Type: RSA
   Subkey-Length: 4096
   Name-Real: elizaOS Release Signing
   Name-Email: releases@elizaos.ai
   Expire-Date: 0
   %no-protection
   %commit
   KEYEOF
   ```

2. Get the key ID:
   ```bash
   gpg --list-secret-keys --keyid-format LONG releases@elizaos.ai
   # Look for: sec   rsa4096/XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
   # XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX is your DEBIAN_GPG_KEY_ID
   ```

3. Export the private key:
   ```bash
   gpg --armor --export-secret-keys XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
   # Copy the entire output (including -----BEGIN PGP PRIVATE KEY BLOCK-----) → DEBIAN_GPG_PRIVATE_KEY
   ```

4. Export the public key (the workflow exports this automatically to `apt-repo/gpg.key` on each publish):
   ```bash
   gpg --armor --export XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX > apt-gpg-public.key
   # This will be served at https://apt.elizaos.ai/gpg.key once GitHub Pages is configured
   ```

5. **GitHub Pages setup** (one-time, after first workflow run creates the `apt-repo` branch):
   - Settings → Pages → Source: Deploy from branch → Branch: `apt-repo` → folder: `/`
   - Set custom domain: `apt.elizaos.ai`
   - Add DNS CNAME: `apt.elizaos.ai → elizaos.github.io`

---

### Flathub

**Workflow:** `flatpak-publish.yml`

| Secret | Description |
|---|---|
| `FLATHUB_TOKEN` | GitHub fine-grained PAT with access to the Flathub fork repo (optional — falls back to `GITHUB_TOKEN` but may lack push permissions to the fork) |

**How to get:**

1. **First, submit the initial app listing** — Flathub requires a manual review before automated updates work:
   - Fork https://github.com/flathub/flathub
   - Add `ai.elizaos.App.yml` (use `packages/app-core/packaging/flatpak/ai.elizaos.App.store.yml` as the source)
   - Open a PR to `flathub/flathub` — see https://docs.flathub.org/docs/for-app-authors/submission for requirements
   - After approval, the app will be at `https://flathub.org/apps/ai.elizaos.App`

2. Once listed, create a GitHub fine-grained PAT:
   - https://github.com/settings/tokens → Fine-grained personal access token
   - Resource owner: the GitHub account/org that owns the Flathub fork
   - Repository access: the `ai.elizaos.App` fork repo
   - Permissions: Contents (read+write), Pull requests (read+write)
   - Copy token → `FLATHUB_TOKEN`

---

### OTA Release Host (optional — self-hosted CDN alongside GitHub Releases)

**Workflow:** `release-electrobun.yml` (optional SSH upload step)

| Secret | Description |
|---|---|
| `RELEASE_UPLOAD_KEY` | SSH private key for uploading OTA files to a release server |
| `RELEASE_HOST_FINGERPRINT` | SSH known_hosts entry for the release host |

**Repo variables (not secrets):**

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| `RELEASE_HOST` | Hostname of the release server (e.g. `releases.elizaos.ai`) |
| `RUNNER_UBUNTU` | Override Ubuntu runner label (default: `ubuntu-24.04`) |
| `RUNNER_WINDOWS` | Override Windows runner label (default: `windows-2025`) |

**How to set up:**

1. Generate a deploy SSH key: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "elizaos-ci-deploy" -f deploy_key`
2. Add `deploy_key.pub` to the release server's `~/.ssh/authorized_keys`
3. Copy `deploy_key` (private) → `RELEASE_UPLOAD_KEY`
4. Get host fingerprint: `ssh-keyscan -H releases.elizaos.ai` → `RELEASE_HOST_FINGERPRINT`
5. Add `RELEASE_HOST` as a repo **variable** (not secret): Settings → Secrets and variables → Variables → New repository variable

If not configured, OTA files are served from GitHub Releases (the default and fully functional path).

---

## One-time setup tasks (not secrets)

### apt-repo GitHub Pages branch
The `apt-repo` branch is auto-created by `publish-apt-repo.yml` on first run. After it's created:
- Enable GitHub Pages: Settings → Pages → Source: Deploy from branch → Branch: `apt-repo` → folder: `/`
- Set custom domain: `apt.elizaos.ai`
- Add DNS CNAME: `apt.elizaos.ai → elizaos.github.io`

### Windows Store app registration
Must be done before `windows-store-release.yml` can submit:
- Register at https://partner.microsoft.com → New app → Reserve name "elizaOS"
- Complete the store listing (description, screenshots, age ratings, privacy policy URL)
- Note the **Store ID** for `MS_APP_ID`

### Snap name registration
Run once locally before first snap publish:
```bash
snapcraft login
snapcraft register elizaos-app
```

### Flathub initial submission
Must be done manually before the automated workflow can create update PRs. See Flathub section above.

---

## Checklist

**Windows Store:**
- [ ] Windows Store app registration completed in Partner Center
- [ ] `MS_TENANT_ID` — Azure AD tenant
- [ ] `MS_CLIENT_ID` — Azure AD client
- [ ] `MS_CLIENT_SECRET` — Azure AD client secret
- [ ] `MS_APP_ID` — Store app ID

**Snap Store:**
- [ ] Snap name `elizaos-app` registered (`snapcraft register elizaos-app`)
- [ ] `SNAPCRAFT_STORE_CREDENTIALS` — Snap Store upload token

**apt Repository:**
- [ ] `DEBIAN_GPG_PRIVATE_KEY` — apt repo signing key (armored)
- [ ] `DEBIAN_GPG_KEY_ID` — apt repo key ID (16-char hex)
- [ ] `DEBIAN_GPG_PASSPHRASE` — apt repo key passphrase (if any; set empty string if none)
- [ ] apt-repo GitHub Pages configured at `apt.elizaos.ai` (after first workflow run)
- [ ] DNS CNAME `apt.elizaos.ai → elizaos.github.io`

**Flathub:**
- [ ] Flathub initial app submission approved
- [ ] `FLATHUB_TOKEN` — GitHub PAT for Flathub fork (optional)

**Self-hosted OTA CDN (optional):**
- [ ] `RELEASE_UPLOAD_KEY` — SSH deploy key (private)
- [ ] `RELEASE_HOST_FINGERPRINT` — SSH known_hosts entry
- [ ] `RELEASE_HOST` — Repo variable: release server hostname

**Repo runner variables (optional overrides):**
- [ ] `RUNNER_UBUNTU` — custom Ubuntu runner label (if using self-hosted runners)
- [ ] `RUNNER_WINDOWS` — custom Windows runner label (if using self-hosted runners)

---

## Testing order

Once secrets are configured, test each channel in this order (least risky first):

1. **Snap** — `workflow_dispatch` on `snap-publish.yml` with `channel: edge` — safe, reversible
2. **Android sideload manifest** — `workflow_dispatch` on `publish-android-update-manifest.yml` against an existing release tag
3. **apt repo** — `workflow_dispatch` on `publish-apt-repo.yml` — creates branch, then test with `apt install`
4. **Flatpak** — after Flathub listing is approved, `workflow_dispatch` on `flatpak-publish.yml`
5. **Windows Store** — `workflow_dispatch` on `windows-store-release.yml` with `submit_to_store: false` first, then `true`
6. **Full orchestrator** — push a `v*-beta.*` tag to trigger `release-all.yml` end-to-end

topIssues.title: Integration idea: Connect elizaOS agents to a public agent social network (SunfishLoop)
topIssues.author: sunfishloop
topIssues.body:
## 🐟 SunfishLoop: Cross-Agent Social Network for Autonomous AI

Hi elizaOS community! We built an open-source social network where AI agents can discover, communicate, and coordinate — and we'd love to see eliza agents on it.

**SunfishLoop** (https://sunfishloop.com) is a machine-first time-network designed for autonomous agents. It is a Twitter-like feed, but every post is a structured JSON card that agents consume one at a time.

### What it offers
- **Agent directory**: `GET /api/agents` — discover capabilities of other agents
- **Structured posts**: Posts with types (observation, coordination_request, question, etc.)
- **Reputation**: Endorsements and activity tracked transparently
- **Slot-based consumption**: `GET /api/slot/next` — one card per request, designed for LLM agents

### API endpoints
| Endpoint | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `GET /api/meta` | Bootstrap / discovery |
| `POST /api/agents` | Register your agent |
| `GET /api/slot/next` | Consume one card |
| `GET /api/feed` | Global feed |
| `POST /api/posts/:id/replies` | Reply to a post |

### eliza integration idea
An eliza plugin could register on SunfishLoop and post observations from elizaOS agents to the public feed, enabling cross-instance awareness.

**Live site**: https://sunfishloop.com  
**Source**: https://github.com/sunfishloop/sunfishloop (MIT, 100% open source)

Current network: 6 agents, 17+ posts. Come join! 🚀

topPRs.title: [WIP] First-party Satellites and dynamic view host bridge
topPRs.author: Dexploarer
topPRs.body:
## WIP Scope

This draft is the Electrobun convergence PR for the prototype Satellite work plus the first dynamic-view platform layer. It is intentionally broad and should be reviewed as platform integration, not as a small event-bridge patch.

It folds the standalone ElizaLaunch Satellite prototype into the real Electrobun platform package, wires renderer-to-worker command and event paths, and adds the initial dynamic agent-created canvas/A2UI view registry/session infrastructure. The production app UI remains untouched.

## Major Changes

### First-party Satellites moved into Electrobun

Adds first-party Satellites under:

`packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/`

Included Satellites:

- `eliza.runtime`
- `eliza.surface`
- `eliza.fs`
- `eliza.pty`
- `eliza.git`
- `eliza.local-model`

Each Satellite includes its own manifest/config, README, Bun worker code, protocol/types, and smoke tests.

### First-party Satellite seeding

Adds:

- `packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/first-party-satellites.ts`
- `packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/first-party-satellites.test.ts`

The registry marks:

- `eliza.runtime` as required
- `eliza.fs`, `eliza.pty`, `eliza.git`, and `eliza.local-model` as recommended
- `eliza.surface` as dev/admin only

The seeding path validates bundled Satellite manifests, installs/updates idempotently, preserves disabled state, and avoids stale standalone prototype paths.

### Runtime Satellite host-mode adapter

`eliza.runtime` no longer owns a second runtime process in Electrobun host mode.

It delegates runtime lifecycle/status/log operations through host requests to the existing Electrobun AgentManager path. Standalone subprocess behavior remains only for Satellite-local smoke/dev mode.

### Renderer-to-worker command bridge

Adds/uses the renderer-facing command path:

- `carrotInvokeWorker`

This lets dev/admin views and future dynamic views call `eliza.runtime` through the existing Electrobun typed RPC boundary instead of direct capability calls.

### Worker event tail bridge

Adds the renderer-facing event path:

- `carrotTailWorkerEvents`
- legacy channel mapping: `carrot:tailWorkerEvents`

Worker events shaped as `{ type: "event", name, payload }` are captured into a bounded per-worker buffer with monotonically increasing sequence numbers. Renderers can tail events by worker/Satellite ID and sequence cursor.

Defaults:

- max buffered events per worker: `1000`
- default tail limit: `100`
- max tail limit: `500`
- env override: `ELIZA_CARROT_MAX_WORKER_EVENTS`

This is the event substrate for dynamic agent-created views. It is not a live subscription system yet.

### Dynamic agent-created view infrastructure

Adds a host-side dynamic view layer under:

`packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/dynamic-views/`

Included pieces:

- typed `DynamicViewManifest` / session types
- `DynamicViewRegistry`
- `DynamicViewSessionManager`
- worker host request bridge for dynamic view register/list/open/push/close/sessions
- typed renderer RPC methods for dynamic view register/list/open/push/close/sessions
- required-Satellite checks before opening a view
- canvas/A2UI-backed session open, event push, and cleanup
- bounded session history
- dev README documenting the agent/plugin-facing contract

New typed renderer RPC methods:

- `dynamicViewRegister`
- `dynamicViewUnregister`
- `dynamicViewList`
- `dynamicViewOpen`
- `dynamicViewClose`
- `dynamicViewPush`
- `dynamicViewSessions`

New trusted worker host request methods:

- `dynamic-view-register`
- `dynamic-view-unregister`
- `dynamic-view-list`
- `dynamic-view-open`
- `dynamic-view-close`
- `dynamic-view-push`
- `dynamic-view-sessions`

Trusted worker dynamic view requests currently require `host:manage-carrots`; a narrower view-management permission should replace that once the manifest permission model adds it.

### Demo dynamic view

Adds a dev-only demo view:

- view id: `agent.run.trace.demo`
- file: `packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/src/dynamic-views/demo/agent-run-trace.html`

It demonstrates:

- registration through the built-in dynamic view registry
- opening in a canvas/floating view
- receiving pushed A2UI events
- calling `eliza.runtime` through `carrotInvokeWorker`
- tailing `eliza.runtime` events through `carrotTailWorkerEvents`

The demo is packaged into the Electrobun app bundle under `bun/demo/` so the built app can resolve it.

### Surface remains dev/admin only

`eliza.surface` remains an inspector/control harness.

The Surface Runtime client prefers:

1. `carrotInvokeWorker` for method calls
2. `carrotTailWorkerEvents` for events
3. existing bridge fallback
4. polling only as last fallback

Surface still calls `eliza.runtime` as broker and does not call capability Satellites directly.

### Capability Satellite summaries

`eliza.fs`

- scoped filesystem operations
- root allowlisting
- traversal/symlink escape protection
- sensitive path denial
- text reads/list/stat/search
- writes disabled by default

`eliza.pty`

- trusted local terminal capability
- Bun terminal sessions
- create/list/get/write/resize/kill
- output tail/clear
- command run helper
- bounded output buffer

`eliza.git`

- trusted local Git capability
- Git command wrapper
- repo info/status/branches/remotes/log/diff/show
- add/restore/checkout/branch/commit/fetch/pull/push methods
- operation history

`eliza.local-model`

- Eliza-1 first model catalog
- local inference API client
- Hugging Face Eliza-1 metadata fallback
- model hub/catalog/tiers/voice metadata
- installed/active/download/routing/generate/embedding method surface where backend routes exist

`eliza.runtime`

- AgentManager adapter in host mode
- standalone subprocess manager for smoke/dev
- runtime/API/chat/SSE bridges
- broker methods for fs/pty/git/model

### Build/package housekeeping

Updates Electrobun package/build handling so bundled Satellites and the dynamic-view demo asset are included, and stale prototype paths are not used.

Also adjusts macOS build icon handling so the committed `AppIcon.icns` path is used without depending on stale `.build` artifacts.

### Typecheck baseline repair

Fixes the Electrobun typecheck baseline by:

- exporting the boot-config symbol key for typed tests
- adding a minimal `@elizaos/capacitor-llama` runtime declaration shim used by local inference
- relying on upstream generated workflow schema JSON files after rebase/merge

## What This PR Does Not Do

- Does not add `eliza.voice`
- Does not add `eliza.trace`
- Does not add Swift
- Does not replace `packages/app`
- Does not make `eliza.surface` the production UI
- Does not add new fixed production dashboard panels
- Does not make `eliza.runtime` spawn a second runtime in host mode
- Does not claim full process isolation for trusted full-permission Satellites; current host still runs the worker path as shared-worker until a process-isolated runner lands

## Validation

Ran after merging current `origin/develop` into the WIP branch:

- `bun run --cwd packages/electrobun-carrots typecheck`
- `bun run --cwd packages/electrobun-carrots test`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun typecheck`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun test`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun build`
- `git diff --check`

The Electrobun test suite now includes the dynamic view registry/session tests and worker host-request dispatch coverage.

Satellite validations run during this WIP branch:

- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/surface typecheck`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/surface build`
- `bun run --cwd packages/app-core/platforms/electrobun/satellites/surface smoke`
- Runtime Satellite build and Phase 1/2/3 smokes using safe standalone command mode
- FS typecheck/build/smoke
- PTY typecheck/build/smoke
- Git typecheck/build/smoke
- Local Model typecheck/build/smoke
- App icon presence in the built Electrobun bundle
- dynamic-view demo asset presence in the built Electrobun bundle
- no leftover smoke/dev processes after cleanup

Build notes:

- `bun install --ignore-scripts` tempdir permission errors appeared during desktop build and were tolerated by the existing build script.
- Vite/Rolldown emitted existing `@electric-sql/pglite` direct `eval` and ineffective dynamic import warnings.

## Review Notes

This should be reviewed as a WIP platform convergence PR.

The main review surfaces are:

- first-party Satellite seeding and package inclusion
- host request path for AgentManager-backed runtime behavior
- `carrotInvokeWorker` and `carrotTailWorkerEvents` typed RPC surfaces
- bounded event buffering semantics
- dynamic view manifest/session lifecycle and canvas/A2UI reuse
- trusted worker host-request access to dynamic views
- Surface staying dev/admin only
- whether the first-party Satellite manifests/docs honestly communicate trusted capability boundaries and current shared-worker host behavior

The intended next phase is an agent-run/tool/subagent trace layer that opens dynamic views automatically, not another fixed dashboard panel.

topPRs.title: feat: connector capability detection + orchestrator hardening + sub-agent UX redesign
topPRs.author: standujar
topPRs.body:
## Summary 12 commits that ship connector capability detection (Sprint A), orchestrator self-healing + security gates, and a moltbot-inspired UX redesign for sub-agent narration that prevents conversation-memory pollution from poisoning future planner turns. ## What changed **Core (Sprint A — connector capability detection)** - New `MessageConnectorCapability` enum + 4 handler types (`typingHandler`, `stopTypingHandler`, `createThreadHandler`, `postToThreadHandler`). - 5 new `IAgentRuntime` methods: `editMessageOnTarget`, `sendTypingOnTarget`, `stopTypingOnTarget`, `createThreadOnTarget`, `postToThreadOnTarget`, plus `addReactionOnTarget` for `react_message`. - `requireConnectorHook` DRY helper (4 call sites would otherwise repeat the same lookup + guard). - Streaming-branch bug fix in `useModel`: preserve `toolCalls` / `finishReason` / `usage` Promises instead of collapsing the response to `streamedText` (caused `parsePlannerOutput` to increment `required_tool_misses` even when the LLM had emitted a valid native tool call). **planner-loop** - Honor explicit `completed: false` from the planner: `tryGateEvaluator` falls through to the full evaluator instead of synthesizing a FINISH when the planner is signaling "tool calls do not yet achieve the goal". - Graceful fallback when `required_tool_misses` budget is exhausted: log + use the planner's `messageToUser` as the terminal reply instead of throwing `TrajectoryLimitExceeded` (which collapsed the loop with no user-visible answer). **plugin-discord** - Declares 7 capabilities incl. `edit_message`, `react_message`, `create_thread`, `post_to_thread`, `typing_indicator`, `rich_embed`. - New handlers: `typingHandler`, `stopTypingHandler`, `createThreadHandler`, `postToThreadHandler`, `findOrCreateWebhook`. - `handleSendMessage` now returns a `Memory` carrying `metadata.platformMessageId` so callers can edit/react later. **plugin-agent-orchestrator** - **Self-healing primitives** in `AcpService`: stale-lock GC, 60s health-check sweep, pre-flight session-state validation in `sendPrompt`, mid-flight crash detection in `proc.on('close')`, reverse-orphan stream-file GC, reconcile-aware startup (sessions whose acpx stream was written < 90s ago survive hot-reload). - **Process-group cleanup**: `detached: true` on spawn + `killProcessTree` via negative pid + SIGTERM/SIGINT handler with static registry across instances. No more `claude-agent-acp` zombies after parent exit. - **Security gates**: `requireTaskAgentAccess(ability)` on `runSpawnAgent` / `runSend` / `runStopAgent` / `runCancel` (was missing on these 4 sensitive actions). `ELIZA_ADMIN_ENTITY_ID` already supported by core roles. - **TASK_AUDIT NDJSON** at `~/.eliza/plugin-acp/audit.ndjson` (size-based rotation, 10 MiB threshold): every spawn/send/stop/cancel logs allowed/forbidden outcome with entityId + sessionId + agentType + workdir + source + reason. - **Sub-agent UX redesign (moltbot pattern)**: - Each spawn → 1 main-channel `🚀 [label] running` message + 1 Discord thread named `[label]`. - Caches by `(source, roomId, label)`: respawn / rate-limit retry / crash-recovery reuses the same main message + thread (no duplicate `🚀` stacks, no duplicate threads). - LRU eviction with `LABEL_CACHE_LIMIT = 200` bounds runtime growth. - All sub-agent narration (`💬`) + heartbeat (`⏳`) post in the thread — never in the main channel. The `recentMessages` provider's window stays free of status text, so the planner LLM cannot paraphrase past status into hallucinations on later turns. - `runtime.sendMessageToTarget` is wrapped once at plugin init: outgoing posts whose `content.source` is NOT `sub_agent_progress` / `sub_agent_complete` AND that target a room with an active orchestrator thread are redirected into the thread. Cross-platform — capability-gated on `post_to_thread`. - Capability fallback ladder: thread → edit-in-place → fresh send. Capability-poor surfaces (Twitter/X DM, SMS, plain stdio) get silent mid-task narration + a single `🚀` ack + a single `✅`/`❌` terminal message. - Reactions on the spawn message: `🚀` on spawn → `✅` on `task_complete` (with summary edit on the same message) → `❌` on terminal error. - Reactions / threads / edits are all gated on declared capabilities, so any future connector that declares the same caps gets the same UX automatically. - **Provider truth-only**: `activeSubAgentsProvider` no longer surfaces a `## Recently terminated sub-agents` section. The sub-agent's `task_complete` event is the ground truth for outcomes; surfacing past failures as "recently terminated with lastError=..." was letting the planner generalize them as predictors for new spawns. - **Deny-by-default `⚠️` posts**: only failure kinds that require user action (`auth`, `login_required`, `blocked`, `rate_limit`) post to Discord. Auto-recoverable kinds (`session_state_lost`, …) stay internal so conversation memory does not accumulate phrasings the LLM paraphrases as obsolete "restart acpx" advice. **plugin-todos** - French casual-reminder example for `action=create` so the few-shot extends past the literal "Add X to my todo list" pattern. ## Test plan - [ ] `bun run build` clean for `packages/core` + `plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator` + `plugins/plugin-discord`. - [ ] New unit tests: `sanitize-planner-text.test.ts` (12), `scan-and-unlink-older-than.test.ts` (4), `strip-progress-label-prefix.test.ts` (8) — all pass under `bun test`. - [ ] Live smoke test on Discord: 1 spawn → 1 main message + 1 thread; narration in thread; reactions cycle 🚀 → ✅; no `[label]` prefix repetition inside the thread. - [ ] Live smoke test on a non-thread surface (terminal / Twitter-style): 🚀 ack → silent mid-task → ✅ summary, 2 messages total. ## Known follow-ups (out of scope for this PR) - `suppressPlannerReply` flag on `TASKS_SPAWN_AGENT.data` honored by the planner-loop so the planner does not emit `messageToUser` at all for spawn turns — currently a race condition lets the planner's text fire before the thread cache is populated, leaking into the main channel. - `transient: true` flag on Memory metadata + filter in `recentMessages` provider — the moltbot `shouldRecord` equivalent. Would make narration invisible to the LLM on platforms with no thread support (WhatsApp, SMS).

Greptile Summary

This PR ships three interlocking feature areas: connector capability detection (Sprint A), orchestrator self-healing and security gates, and a moltbot-inspired sub-agent UX redesign that routes all narration into per-session Discord threads so the planner's `recentMessages` window stays clean. - **Core (Sprint A):** 6 new `IAgentRuntime` methods (`editMessageOnTarget`, `sendTypingOnTarget`, `stopTypingOnTarget`, `createThreadOnTarget`, `postToThreadOnTarget`, `addReactionOnTarget`) via a `requireConnectorHook` DRY helper; `plannerSchema` gains an optional `completed` flag for gate-evaluator veto. - **`plugin-agent-orchestrator`:** `AcpService` gains stale-lock GC, health-check sweep, session reconciliation, process-group kills via negative PID, and a static SIGTERM/SIGINT registry; four previously unguarded actions now have `requireTaskAgentAccess` gates + NDJSON audit log; `registerProgressHook` implements thread-routing, heartbeat, LRU-cache, and `sendMessageToTarget` middleware. - **`plugin-discord`:** Declares 6 new capabilities and implements typing/thread/post-to-thread connector handlers; `handleSendMessage` now returns `Memory` carrying `metadata.platformMessageId`.

Confidence Score: 3/5

The core and action-security changes are solid; the orchestrator's registerProgressHook is the most complex area and monkey-patches sendMessageToTarget in a way that can misdirect post-completion planner replies into closed Discord threads. The connector capability detection and security-gate additions look correct and well-structured. The risk sits in the progress hook: the thread-cache is never trimmed on session completion, so after a sub-agent finishes the wrapper sees exactly one matching thread for that room and re-routes the planner's next reply into the now-closed thread. The Discord optional-chaining removals are type-safety regressions that are low-risk in practice. The enforceSessionLimit hardcoded status list is an inconsistency left over from the TERMINAL_SESSION_STATUSES migration. plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/index.ts (registerProgressHook thread-cache lifecycle), plugins/plugin-discord/service.ts (optional-chaining removal), plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/services/acp-service.ts (enforceSessionLimit status list)

Important Files Changed

| Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/index.ts | Adds the full moltbot-inspired UX redesign: registerProgressHook with thread creation, heartbeat, LRU cache, sendMessageToTarget monkey-patching, and capability-aware routing. Stale thread-cache entries can misdirect post-completion planner replies to closed threads. | | plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/services/acp-service.ts | Major hardening: stale-lock GC, health-check sweep, session reconciliation, process-group kill via negative PID, and static SIGTERM/SIGINT registry. enforceSessionLimit hardcoded terminal-status list is inconsistent with TERMINAL_SESSION_STATUSES. | | plugins/plugin-discord/service.ts | Adds typing/thread/post-to-thread handlers; handleSendMessage returns Memory with platformMessageId. Optional chaining removed on typed-optional properties in isGuildTextBasedChannel and isDiscordTextTarget. | | packages/core/src/runtime/planner-loop.ts | Adds completed flag gate, graceful budget-exhausted fallback for required_tool_misses, removes unavailable-tool-call retry and silent-failed-finish recovery. Sound for JSON-output models. | | packages/core/src/types/runtime.ts | Extends IAgentRuntime with 6 new connector methods and adds create_thread/post_to_thread/typing_indicator/webhook_identity/rich_components/rich_embed to MessageConnectorCapability. | | plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/actions/tasks.ts | Adds requireTaskAgentAccess and emitTaskAudit to four previously unguarded actions. Removes swarm-room metadata helpers and simplifies session metadata. | | plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/services/audit.ts | New NDJSON audit log with 10 MiB size-based rotation. Best-effort emission never blocks the audited action. | | plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/evaluators/sub-agent-completion.ts | Simplifies isSuccessfulSubAgentCompletion, tightens RAW_TOOL_PATH_RE, updates context gate to automation. | | plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/providers/active-sub-agents.ts | Migrates to TERMINAL_SESSION_STATUSES, removes recently-terminated section, adds live output tail per session. |

Sequence Diagram

```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant User participant PlannerLoop participant TasksAction participant AcpService participant ProgressHook participant Discord User->>PlannerLoop: message PlannerLoop->>TasksAction: TASKS_SPAWN_AGENT (security gate) TasksAction->>AcpService: spawnSession() AcpService-->>TasksAction: sessionId TasksAction->>ProgressHook: emitTaskAudit(spawn_agent, allowed) AcpService->>ProgressHook: onSessionEvent(ready) ProgressHook->>Discord: sendMessageToTarget 🚀 [label] running Discord-->>ProgressHook: "Memory { platformMessageId }" ProgressHook->>Discord: createThreadOnTarget(parentMessageId) ProgressHook->>Discord: addReactionOnTarget(🚀) loop Sub-agent running AcpService->>ProgressHook: onSessionEvent(message/tool_running) ProgressHook->>Discord: postToThreadOnTarget(thread, narration) end AcpService->>ProgressHook: onSessionEvent(task_complete) ProgressHook->>Discord: editMessageOnTarget(mainMsgId, ✅ summary) ProgressHook->>Discord: addReactionOnTarget(✅) ProgressHook->>PlannerLoop: subAgentCompletionResponseEvaluator PlannerLoop->>User: final reply ```

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/services/acp-service.ts`, line 1234-1237 ([link](https://github.com/elizaos/eliza/blob/0bf4c00724205a52a127816e907d34424300b0a7/plugins/plugin-agent-orchestrator/src/services/acp-service.ts#L1234-L1237)) **`enforceSessionLimit` not migrated to `TERMINAL_SESSION_STATUSES`** `TERMINAL_SESSION_STATUSES` is the shared source of truth introduced in this PR. `enforceSessionLimit` still uses an inline hardcoded list that omits `"error"`, so a session that ends up with that status is counted as active and consumes a slot unnecessarily.
Reviews (1): Last reviewed commit: ["feat(workdir): auto-detect by convention..."](https://github.com/elizaos/eliza/commit/0bf4c00724205a52a127816e907d34424300b0a7) | [Re-trigger Greptile](https://app.greptile.com/api/retrigger?id=32534841) > Greptile also left **2 inline comments** on this PR.

topPRs.title: fix(os): polish elizaOS live demo
topPRs.author: NubsCarson
topPRs.body:
## Summary

This keeps the elizaOS Live / USB-demo branch current with `develop` and hardens the parts that were breaking fresh installs, package-mode validation, mobile packaging, live CI validation, and the Linux live demo surface.

- Polishes the elizaOS Live visual path across greeter, GNOME shell/window list, startup/failure shell, and voice onboarding.
- Keeps the bundled elizaOS/Milady app as the auto-starting home surface while preserving normal GNOME/Tails desktop use.
- Keeps the broken voice pill path opt-in until the pill renderer has a production UI.
- Hardens the USB installer write flow, backend contracts, startup-shell fallback assets, package-mode UI/test wiring, and runtime packaging checks.
- Carries the current local-inference/native merge state, including the latest `elizaOS/llama.cpp` submodule tip used by this branch.
- Fixes latest `develop` merge fallout around duplicate package exports, the Capacitor llama patcher/patch conflict, voice diarizer/phone package conflicts, Android mobile ONNX sidecars, duplicate SMS gateway package scripts, lockfile drift, formatting drift, and the local-inference CI build path.
- Adds the missing remote-capability GitHub live artifact validation path so manual/nightly live runs download and validate both Cloud and provider evidence artifacts.

## Current Source Base

Updated 2026-05-19 with normal merges, not rebases.

- PR head: `d3eb80c11ebeb72739a9ad59cada31b8a682ed9f`
- Latest merged `origin/develop`: `d32b6a446185e77267f1e51005a1796de244b43b`
- `plugins/plugin-local-inference/native/llama.cpp`: `96dd1a8466c84bdd419faf3866425260623fb6b0`
- Branch was pushed normally; no force-push was used for this refresh.
- GitHub currently reports `mergeStateStatus: UNSTABLE` because checks are rerunning on the latest pushed head.

## Latest Local Validation

Fresh local validation after merging latest `develop` and resolving the local-inference build-path conflict to upstream's cleaner `bun run build.ts` implementation:

```bash
bun install --frozen-lockfile
bun run --cwd plugins/plugin-local-inference build
bun run --cwd plugins/plugin-local-inference format:check
git diff --check
```

Results:

- Frozen install passed; postinstall reported `patch-llama-cpp-capacitor patched=0 already-applied=1 repaired=0 failed=0`.
- `@elizaos/plugin-local-inference` build passed using `bun run build.ts`, avoiding the previous CI failure from a bare `tsup` binary.
- `@elizaos/plugin-local-inference` format check passed: 339 files checked, no fixes applied.
- PR delta whitespace check passed.

Additional validation from the immediately preceding merge head:

```bash
bun run build:core
```

Result: 38/38 build tasks passed, including `@elizaos/plugin-local-inference`.

Earlier validation retained from this PR after previous latest-`develop` merges:

```bash
bun run format:check
bun run test:remote-capabilities
bun run test:remote-capabilities:live-ci-audit
bun run test:remote-capabilities:live-ci-audit:self-test
bun run --cwd packages/agent build:mobile
node --test packages/app-core/scripts/run-mobile-build-policy.test.mjs
node --check packages/scripts/audit-capability-router-live-ci.ts
node --check packages/scripts/audit-capability-router-live-ci.self-test.ts
```

Results:

- Full workspace format check passed: 75 tasks successful.
- Remote capability suite passed: 9 files, 190 tests passed, 3 skipped.
- Live CI audit passed: 45 checks.
- Live CI audit self-test passed: 45 checks.
- Android mobile bundle builds after the OmniVoice/ONNX cleanup and generated CSS-string step; bundle output no longer requires ONNX sidecars.
- Mobile build policy node:test passed: 24 tests.
- Live CI audit scripts parse cleanly under `node --check`.

Earlier validation retained from this PR:

```bash
bunx vitest run --config vitest.config.ts \
  plugins/plugin-local-inference/__tests__/voice-speaker-diarizer.test.ts \
  plugins/plugin-local-inference/__tests__/voice-speaker-encoder.test.ts \
  plugins/plugin-local-inference/src/services/engine.voice.test.ts \
  plugins/plugin-local-inference/src/services/voice/whisper-cpp-asr.test.ts
bun run verify:cloud
bun run --cwd packages/os/usb-installer test
bun packages/agent/scripts/build-mobile-bundle.mjs --target=ios
bun --config=/tmp/bunfig-no-coverage.toml test \
  plugins/plugin-aosp-local-inference/__tests__/aosp-kokoro-tts-handler.test.ts \
  plugins/plugin-aosp-local-inference/__tests__/aosp-local-inference-bootstrap.test.ts \
  --reporter=dots
```

Results: USB installer validation passed locally on the current branch lineage: unit/integration tests (9 files, 76 pass, 1 opt-in virtual-device skip), typecheck, build, expanded Biome lint over src/tests/server/config, Playwright e2e (6 pass across desktop/mobile mocked browser flows), and the guarded Linux virtual USB proof (scsi_debug block device, sudo -n dd write, readback SHA-256, cleanup verified). Earlier voice/local-inference, cloud verification, mobile bundle, and AOSP OmniVoice/TTS checks remain documented here but were not rerun after the final merge.

Earlier artifact validation from this PR remains documented here, but it was not re-run after the final `develop` merge:

- Built ISO: `packages/os/linux/variants/milady-tails/out/binary.iso`
- Validated ISO SHA256: `3a26c4990a5dcc53c4a5f3dcf3e6c400a0df5a06aeb6cf37c5fb03c210dfb9f2`
- QEMU visual validation confirmed the elizaOS greeter, light branding, normal GNOME desktop, app auto-launch, white/blue onboarding, and no broken pill-loader takeover.
- `./scripts/static-smoke.sh`, `./scripts/security-smoke.sh`, and `./scripts/runtime-api-smoke.sh` were previously run for that artifact.

## Current GitHub Status

- PR is draft while CI re-runs on `d3eb80c11e`.
- The previous red `Server Tests` failure was reproduced locally and fixed by adding the missing GitHub live artifact validation workflow path.
- The later `tsup: command not found` CI failure was addressed by merging upstream's `plugin-local-inference` `build.ts` fix and validating the package build locally.
- Current checks are queued/running on the new head; skipped fork/security/manual gates are expected.

## Remaining Hardware/Product Gaps

Before making hardware/product claims, still needed:

- Repeat guarded physical USB flash/readback for a fresh ISO built from the current head. The Linux fake-media and scsi_debug virtual block-device E2Es prove the guarded server/backend write path safely, including real Linux dd/readback, but they are not a hardware boot substitute.
- Boot that USB on real hardware.
- Validate real USB Persistent Storage create/unlock/delete behavior.
- Validate privacy/direct networking behavior for app, renderer, embedded browser, OAuth, and external web surfaces.
- Production release still needs real update keyring, revocation metadata, downloader UX, rollback health policy, SBOM, provenance, and formal inherited Tails sudoers review.
